cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are spending time with one of the most beloved and most useful investing books ever written.
It is called One Up On Wall Street: How To Use What You Already Know To Make Money In The Market, and it was written by Peter Lynch, with help from the financial writer John Rothchild.
If the name Peter Lynch does not mean anything to you yet, let me fix that in one sentence.
Peter Lynch ran the Fidelity Magellan fund from 1977 to 1990, and over those thirteen years he averaged around twenty nine percent a year.
Let that number sit for a moment.
Twenty nine percent, compounded, year after year.
He turned Magellan from a small fund into the largest mutual fund in the world, growing it from about twenty million dollars to fourteen billion.
A thousand dollars left with Lynch at the start would have become around twenty eight thousand by the time he stepped away.
He is, by almost any measure, one of the greatest stock pickers who ever lived.
And here is the astonishing thing about this book.
Lynch spends most of it trying to convince you, an ordinary person with a job and a family and a grocery list, that you can do this too.
Not exactly as well as he did, but far better than you have been told you can.
That is the heart of the book, and I want you to hold onto it the whole way through.
Lynch's central, radical, empowering idea is that the average person, the amateur investor, the customer, actually has a built in edge over the professionals on Wall Street.
Not a disadvantage.
An edge.
You spend your life as a consumer, walking through malls, working inside industries, using products you love, watching what your kids beg for and what your neighbors line up to buy.
And because of that, you spot great companies in everyday life long before the analysts in their glass towers ever get around to noticing them.
Lynch calls this investing in what you know, and he means it almost literally.

The next great stock is very often hiding in plain sight, in your kitchen cabinet, in the parking lot of the store you can never find a space at, in the tool your company just bought that made everyone's job easier.
Let me give you the story that made this idea famous, because it is such a good one.
Lynch's wife, Carolyn, came home from the grocery store one day genuinely excited about a product.

It was a pair of pantyhose called L'eggs, sold in these little plastic egg shaped containers, placed right by the checkout counter in supermarkets and drugstores rather than in some department store across town.
Carolyn loved them.
The quality was good, and you could grab a pair while you were already buying milk and bread.
Lynch paid attention.
He found out that L'eggs was made by a company called Hanes.

He did his homework, and he discovered that the average woman went to a supermarket or drugstore far more often than she went to a department store, which meant Hanes had cracked the distribution problem that the whole industry had missed.
He bought Hanes, and it became one of the great winners of his early career, a stock that multiplied several times over.
And the tip did not come from a Harvard economist or a Wall Street research report.
It came from his wife, at the checkout line, noticing something good.
Lynch collected these kinds of observations everywhere.
He noticed Dunkin' Donuts, a plain, unglamorous company that just sold coffee and donuts, and he liked that it was a business he could understand and see thriving.
He watched Taco Bell.
He stayed at La Quinta Motor Inns, a chain of no frills motels, and noticed how well run and how busy they were, and that turned into a wonderful investment.
He watched his kids and their friends flock to Toys R Us.
His point, again and again, is that the ordinary experiences of your ordinary life are a stream of investment ideas, if only you are paying attention.

The person who works at a chemical plant and notices that a certain resin is suddenly in impossible demand, the nurse who sees which drug is actually working, the store manager who watches one product fly off the shelves while another gathers dust, all of these people are getting information weeks, months, sometimes years before it filters up to Wall Street.
Lynch's whole career is a monument to the idea that this information is worth more than most people think.
But now I need to give you the caveat, because this is where a lot of people who only half read Lynch go badly wrong.
Investing in what you know is only the beginning.
It is the start of the process, not the end.
Noticing a great product, loving a great store, is a lead.
It is a reason to investigate.
It is absolutely not, by itself, a reason to buy the stock.
Lynch is very firm about this.
A great product tells you a company might be worth looking at.

It does not tell you the stock is cheap, it does not tell you the company is drowning in debt, it does not tell you whether the great product is a tiny fraction of a giant company or the whole engine of a small one.
You still have to do the research.
You have to open up the story of the business, look at the earnings, look at the balance sheet, understand how the company actually makes money and whether it can keep growing.
So the full instruction is not simply buy what you know.
It is, use what you know to find promising candidates, and then do the work to find out if the candidate is actually a good investment.
The everyday observation gets you to the starting line faster than the pros.
The research is how you win the race.

Now, Lynch spends a good deal of energy tearing down the wall of intimidation that Wall Street builds around itself, and I want to walk you through this because it will change how you feel about your own ability to do this.
There is a theory, popular in academic circles, called the efficient market hypothesis.

In its strong form it says that all information is already reflected in stock prices, that markets are perfectly rational, and therefore that no amateur, and really no professional either, can consistently beat the market.
Lynch thinks this is nonsense, and he says so with a smile.
His entire track record is an argument against it.
If markets were perfectly efficient, he could not have averaged twenty nine percent a year for thirteen years by simply doing careful homework on companies other people ignored.

He points out that stocks are frequently mispriced, that fear and fashion and neglect leave wonderful companies sitting at silly prices, and that a patient individual who does the work can take advantage of it.
He also wants you to understand why the professionals are often at a disadvantage, which is one of the most liberating parts of the book.
Fund managers, he explains, operate under a thick set of constraints that you, sitting at your kitchen table, do not have.

There is what he calls the street lag, the slow drip of information and permission that means by the time a big institution is finally allowed to buy an exciting small company, the stock has often already tripled.

A professional manager frequently cannot buy a stock until the analysts have blessed it, the research has been published, and the company has grown big enough and respectable enough to be considered safe.

There is the problem of only being allowed to own respectable, big name companies, because a manager has to be able to justify every holding to a committee, and no committee wants to hear that you put client money into some obscure company nobody has heard of.
And there is career risk, which Lynch describes with real honesty.
The famous saying on Wall Street is that you never get fired for buying a big blue chip name even if it collapses, but you can absolutely get fired for buying an unknown company that then goes down.
So managers conform.
They cluster into the same safe stocks, they hug the crowd, and they avoid exactly the small, unglamorous, early stage companies where the biggest gains are waiting.
You have none of these problems.
You answer to no committee.
You can buy the tiny company the day you understand it.
That freedom is your advantage, and Lynch wants you to feel it.
Let me now introduce you to one of the most delightful ideas in the book, the tenbagger.
A tenbagger is a stock that goes up tenfold, a stock you buy and that eventually becomes worth ten times what you paid.
Put in a thousand dollars, get back ten thousand.
Lynch borrowed the word from baseball, where bases are bags, and a tenbagger would be the kind of hit that is off the charts.
And here is the beautiful, freeing part of his argument.
You do not need many of them.
You need a few in a lifetime.

In fact, in a portfolio of a dozen small stocks, you can be wrong on most of them, you can have some go nowhere and some even lose money, and still do wonderfully, because one or two enormous winners can carry the entire portfolio.
If you put equal amounts into ten stocks, the most you can lose on any one is what you put in, but the winners have no ceiling.
A single tenbagger can pay for a whole handful of losers and then some.
This is why Lynch is not afraid of being wrong often.
He is playing a game where the winners can run to the sky and the losers can only fall to zero, and he only needs to find a few big ones.
That should take a lot of pressure off your shoulders.
You do not have to be right all the time.
You have to be right about the big ones, and you have to let them run.
Now we come to the practical backbone of the book, the framework that turns all this inspiration into a real method.
Lynch says that before you can judge a company, you have to know what kind of company it is, because you judge each type by completely different standards.
He sorts every company into one of six categories, and I want to walk through all six with you carefully, because this single idea will make you a smarter investor immediately.
The first category is the slow grower.
These are large, aging companies, often once fast growing but now mature, that expand only a little faster than the overall economy, maybe a few percent a year.

They are typically big, well established, and they usually pay a generous and dependable dividend, because they cannot find enough new places to reinvest their profits, so they hand the cash back to shareholders.
Think of an old, sprawling utility or a long established manufacturer whose glory days of growth are behind it.
Lynch is frank that he does not get very excited about slow growers.
You are not going to find a tenbagger here.
What you look for in a slow grower is a solid, rising dividend that is being paid reliably, and a stock price cheap enough to make that dividend worthwhile.
These are not the stars of your portfolio.
They are the quiet, dividend paying anchors, and Lynch generally keeps only a small allocation to them, if any.
The second category is the stalwart.
This is where you meet the big, powerful, still growing companies, the ones with billions in sales that nonetheless manage to keep growing at a respectable clip, maybe ten to twelve percent a year.
These are your Coca Colas, your Procter and Gambles, the great household names that sell things people use every single day.
You are not going to get a tenbagger from a stalwart very often, because they are simply too big to multiply that fast.
But you can very reasonably expect a good, solid gain, something like thirty to fifty percent, if you buy them at a fair price and are patient.
And here is the crucial role stalwarts play in your portfolio.
They are your defense.
In a recession, when the economy sours and shakier companies are getting crushed, people still buy their soda, their soap, their toothpaste, their groceries.
So stalwarts hold up.
Lynch likes to keep some stalwarts around precisely for this protection.

His approach with a stalwart is often to buy it when it is out of favor and cheap, ride it for that thirty to fifty percent gain, sell it, and then rotate into another one that has fallen out of favor.
He is not marrying these companies.
He is renting them at good prices for reliable, moderate returns and downside protection.
The third category is Lynch's favorite, and it is where the tenbaggers live.
These are the fast growers.
Small, aggressive, new companies, often young, growing their earnings at twenty to twenty five percent a year or even faster.
This is where fortunes are made, because a company that can compound at twenty five percent a year and keep doing it becomes an enormous winner over time.
Lynch loved fast growers.
Hanes was one.

Many of his best investments were small companies in ordinary businesses, not glamorous high technology, but simple things like restaurants, retailers, and hotels that had figured out a formula and were rolling it out across the country.
The key insight Lynch offers here is that a fast grower does not have to be in a fast growing industry.

In fact, he often preferred a fast growing company inside a boring, slow, no growth industry, because in a sleepy industry there is little competition rushing in, and a well run company can keep taking market share and expanding for years.
Now, fast growers are also risky.
They can stumble.
They can run out of room to grow, or expand too quickly and trip over their own feet, or get overpriced by an excited crowd.
So the essential question with a fast grower is always, can it keep growing, and is there still a long runway ahead.
As long as the company keeps producing the growth and the expansion story is still early, you hold on.
When the growth slows and the story is over, or the price has raced far ahead of the earnings, you get out.
This category demands the most attention, but it also pays the most.
The fourth category is the cyclical.
A cyclical is a company whose sales and profits rise and fall in a fairly predictable rhythm with the overall economy.
Think of automobiles, airlines, steel, chemicals, and other heavy industries.
When the economy is booming, people buy new cars, businesses expand, factories run hot, and cyclicals make a lot of money.
When the economy slumps, all of that dries up, and cyclicals can lose money fast.
Lynch warns that cyclicals are the most misunderstood of all the categories, and the place where people most often get hurt, because a cyclical can look deceptively like a stalwart.
It has a familiar name, it has been around forever, it seems safe.
But if you buy a cyclical at the wrong point in its cycle, at the peak, when earnings look fantastic and everyone is optimistic, you can lose half your money as the cycle turns down.
With cyclicals, timing is everything, and you have to actually understand the industry's cycle.

The counterintuitive truth Lynch teaches is that a cyclical can look most expensive, with a very low price to earnings ratio, exactly when it is most dangerous, at the top of the cycle, and look most frightening, with a high ratio or no earnings at all, exactly when it is closest to the bottom and about to recover.
This is where working inside an industry gives you a real edge, because you can see the inventory piling up or the orders starting to flow again before the numbers appear in the newspaper.
The fifth category is the turnaround, and this is where Lynch tells one of his most famous stories.
A turnaround is a company that has been beaten down, battered, sometimes pushed to the very edge of bankruptcy, a company everyone has given up on, that then manages to come back to life.
These are not slow growers, they are the walking wounded, and if you get one right the returns can be spectacular because you buy it when the price reflects near total despair.
Lynch's great example is Chrysler.
In the late 1970s and early 1980s, Chrysler was in terrible trouble, so close to failure that the United States government stepped in to guarantee its loans.
The stock had collapsed.
Most people assumed it was going to die.

But Lynch did his homework, and he saw that Chrysler had a lot of cash on hand, was not going to go bankrupt in the near term, had new products coming, including the minivan that would become a sensation, and had capable leadership.
He invested heavily, and Chrysler recovered dramatically, becoming one of the biggest winners in Magellan.
He also did well with Ford in similar fashion.

The lesson with turnarounds is that they can add wonderful gains and, importantly, they tend to march to their own drummer, rising or falling based on the company's own recovery rather than moving with the general market.
But you must investigate carefully, because a company that looks near death sometimes actually is.
The question is whether it has the balance sheet, the cash, and the plan to survive long enough to recover.
The sixth and final category is the asset play.
This is a company that is quietly sitting on something valuable that the market is either ignoring or does not fully appreciate.
It might be real estate carried on the books at a fraction of its true worth.
It might be a huge pile of cash.
It might be a valuable brand name, or oil and gas reserves, or a subscriber base, or a patent, or even tax losses that can shelter future profits.

The point is that if you know the local situation, if you understand the industry, you can sometimes see hidden value on a balance sheet that the broad market has overlooked, and buy the whole company for less than its parts are worth.
Lynch loved asset plays because they gave you a kind of safety net, a floor of real value underneath the stock.

This is another place where being a local, being an insider to a particular business or town, gives the amateur a real edge over the distant analyst who only sees the reported numbers and never walks the actual land the company owns.
So those are the six, slow growers, stalwarts, fast growers, cyclicals, turnarounds, and asset plays.
And Lynch's instruction is simple but powerful.

Before you do anything else with a company, figure out which category it belongs to, because your expectations, the questions you ask, the price you are willing to pay, and the reasons you would sell are all completely different for each type.
Expecting a slow grower to double is foolish.
Holding a fast grower expecting a steady dividend is missing the point.
Buying a cyclical and treating it like a stalwart you can hold forever can wreck you.
Categorize first, then judge by the right standard.

A company can also move between categories over its life, a fast grower matures into a stalwart, a stalwart stumbles and becomes a turnaround, and part of your job is noticing when that shift happens.
This brings us to a wonderfully practical tool Lynch calls the two minute drill.
Before you buy any stock, he says, you should be able to give a short, clear speech, no more than a couple of minutes, that explains the story of why you own it.
What does the company do, why is it attractive, what has to happen for it to succeed, and what are the pitfalls in its path.

If it is a fast grower, your two minute drill might be, this company grows at twenty five percent a year, it has opened stores in only a few states so far, it has a proven formula, the balance sheet is clean, and if it just keeps rolling that formula out across the country the earnings will keep climbing.
If you cannot explain the story simply, in language a child could follow, then you do not really understand what you own, and you have no business owning it.
This drill is your protection against buying on a tip, on excitement, on a hot rumor.

It forces you to know the actual reasons, and it gives you a clear scorecard, because later, when you check in on the company, you can ask whether the story is still unfolding the way you expected or whether it has changed.
Now let me share Lynch's playful and very useful portrait of the perfect stock, or at least the perfect kind of company to investigate.

He lays out a list of attributes that make him fall in love, and what is funny and instructive about them is that almost all of them are things that would make an ordinary investor turn up their nose.
Lynch loves a company whose very name sounds dull, or even ridiculous, because a boring name scares away the crowd and keeps the price low.
He loves a company that does something dull, some unglamorous little product nobody wants to talk about at a dinner party, because again, the professionals ignore it.

Even better, he loves a company that does something disagreeable, something a little disgusting that makes people wince, like a company that collects grease from restaurants or handles waste, because that keeps the fashionable money far away.
He loves a spinoff, a division that a big parent company has cut loose as a separate stock, because spinoffs are often neglected, misunderstood, and freed to finally perform on their own.
He loves a company that the big institutions do not own and that the analysts do not follow, because that means it has not yet been discovered and bid up, and you are early.

He is delighted by a company surrounded by unpleasant rumors, perhaps whispers of some connection to toxic waste, or even the mafia, because that fear depresses the price of a business that may be perfectly fine underneath.
He likes a company with something depressing about it, something that makes people not want to think about it.
He likes a company in a no growth industry, because everyone avoids no growth industries, and yet a great operator inside one faces little competition and can quietly dominate.

He loves a company with a niche, a franchise or a stranglehold on some corner of a market where no one else can easily compete, like the only gravel pit in a region, or a brand so strong that a rival would need a fortune to challenge it.

He loves a business where people have to keep buying the product over and over, the razor blades and the soft drinks and the drugs and the cigarettes, the repeat purchases that provide a steady, predictable stream of sales rather than a one time sale.

He prefers a company that is a user of technology rather than a maker of it, because when the price of computers or automation falls, the company that uses that technology to cut its costs benefits, without having to fight the brutal, fast moving competition that the technology makers face.

He loves seeing insiders, the executives and directors who know the company best, buying shares with their own money, because there are many reasons an insider might sell a stock but only one reason an insider buys, they think it is going up.

And finally he loves a company that is buying back its own shares, because when a company repurchases its stock it shrinks the number of shares outstanding, which increases every remaining shareholder's slice of the earnings, and it signals that management believes the stock is a good value.
Just as important as what Lynch loves is the list of things he avoids, and these are just as instructive because they are precisely the stocks that beginners are most tempted by.

He warns you to be very careful about the hottest stock in the hottest industry, the one everyone is talking about, the one splashed across the magazine covers, because by the time it is that popular the price already assumes a perfect future, and the crowd rushing in has a way of getting trampled on the way out.

He warns you about any company being sold to you as the next something, the next great tech giant, the next famous winner, because these comparisons almost never come true and the hype inflates the price far beyond the reality.

He coins a marvelous word, diworseification, to describe what happens when a successful company, flush with cash, decides to make foolish acquisitions in businesses it does not understand, spreading itself thin and destroying value instead of building it, worsening itself through diversification for its own sake.
Watch out for that.

He warns about the whisper stock, the exciting story you hear about in hushed tones, the company with the miracle cure or the world changing gadget that has no earnings yet, only a thrilling tale, because these stories are seductive and usually end in heartbreak.

And he warns about a company that is overly dependent on a single customer, because if a huge chunk of a supplier's sales go to just one big buyer, that buyer holds all the power and can squeeze the supplier's profits or walk away and cripple the business overnight.
Now let us get into the numbers, because as I told you, the everyday observation is only the lead, and the research is how you win.
Lynch is not a man who buries you in complicated formulas, but he insists on checking a handful of key figures, and I want to give them to you clearly.
The most important number is the price to earnings ratio, the P E ratio, which is simply the price of the stock divided by the company's earnings per share.
It tells you how many years of current earnings you are paying for.
A crucial habit Lynch teaches is to never look at a P E ratio in isolation, but always to compare it to the company's growth rate.
His famous rule of thumb is that the P E ratio of a fairly priced company should roughly equal its growth rate.
So a company growing earnings at twenty percent a year is reasonably priced at a P E of twenty, a bargain at a P E of ten, and expensive at a P E of forty.
This is the seed of what later investors named the PEG ratio, the price to earnings ratio divided by the growth rate.
If that number is well below one, you may have found a bargain, a company growing much faster than its price suggests.
If it is well above one, you are probably overpaying.
This one simple comparison, price versus growth, will save you from countless expensive mistakes and help you spot genuine bargains that the crowd has missed.
There are several other numbers Lynch wants you to check.
Look at the percentage of the company owned by big institutions, and remember that with Lynch, less is better.
If the institutions own almost none of it and no analyst covers it, that is a good sign, it means you are early, before the herd arrives.
Look at whether insiders are buying shares and whether the company is buying back its own stock, both bullish signals as we discussed.
Study the earnings growth record over several years, because earnings are what ultimately drive a stock, and you want to see consistent, real growth, not a wild, erratic zigzag.

Check the cash position, how much cash the company is holding, because a rich cash pile provides safety and options, and in Lynch's Chrysler analysis the cash on the books was a big part of why he knew the company would survive.
Look hard at the balance sheet, specifically the debt to equity ratio, the balance between what the company owes and what the shareholders own.
A company with little debt has staying power, it can survive a downturn and a mistake.
A company buried in debt is fragile, and in a turnaround situation especially, the debt load can be the difference between recovery and collapse.

Look at dividends, and whether the company has a long, unbroken record of paying and raising them, which for slow growers and stalwarts is a sign of discipline and stability, and gives you something to hold onto while you wait.

And watch inventories very carefully, because inventories piling up faster than sales is a serious red flag, a signal that the company's products are not selling as expected and that trouble is coming, whereas inventories being drawn down can be a hopeful sign for a turnaround.

Lynch even mentions the book value trap, the temptation to buy a stock just because it trades below its stated book value, and he cautions that book value can be badly misleading, because the assets on the books may be worth far less than the accountants say, old inventory, obsolete factories, receivables that will never be collected, so you must ask what the assets are really, truly worth, not just what the balance sheet claims.
Lynch also gives down to earth advice on building and running a portfolio.
He is not a fan of over diversifying just for the sake of it, the practice of buying a huge number of stocks you cannot possibly follow.
He suggests owning as many good companies as you can find that meet your standards and that you can realistically keep up with, and no more.
For a typical individual, that might be a handful, perhaps three to ten stocks that you genuinely understand and monitor.

He suggests spreading across the categories to balance risk and reward, keeping some stalwarts for defense, some fast growers for the big upside, maybe a turnaround or an asset play where you have a real edge.

The important thing is that you actually follow each company you own, checking in every few months to see whether its story is still on track, because owning a stock is not a one time decision but an ongoing relationship.
And he reminds you to keep new money flowing in and to think in terms of years, not days, because the great gains take time to develop.
On the question of when to buy and when to sell, Lynch offers some of the most valuable and hardest to follow advice in the entire book.

On buying, he likes moments when good companies are cheap because the whole market has fallen and dragged everything down, or because a particular company has hit a temporary, fixable problem that has scared everyone away.
Fear creates bargains.
On selling, his guiding principle is beautifully simple, you sell when the story has fundamentally changed, not when the price wiggles or the news anchor sounds worried.
For each category there is a right reason to sell.
You sell a fast grower when its growth is clearly slowing and its long runway has run out, or when its price has raced so far ahead of its earnings that even continued success cannot justify it.
You sell a stalwart after it has delivered your thirty to fifty percent gain and rotate into another that is cheaper.
You sell a cyclical when the cycle is turning down, when inventories are building and the boom is ending.
You sell a turnaround once it has fully recovered and become a healthy company again, because the easy, dramatic gains from despair to normal are behind it.
You sell an asset play when the hidden asset has been recognized by the market and the value has been unlocked.
In every case the trigger is a real change in the underlying business story, something you can point to, not a scary headline or a bad day on the market.
And this leads us to what may be the single most important discipline Lynch tries to teach you, the discipline of ignoring the noise.

He wants you to tune out the endless stream of economic predictions, the forecasts about interest rates and recessions and where the market is headed next quarter, because nobody, not even the experts, can reliably predict these things, and trying to trade around them will only hurt you.
He famously said that if you spend more than a few minutes a year worrying about the direction of the economy, you have wasted a lot of time.

He wants you to ignore the daily swings of the market, the up two hundred points, down two hundred points drama that fills the news, because for a long term investor in good companies, that daily churn is meaningless static.
What matters is whether your companies are growing their earnings and executing their stories.
The market will follow the earnings eventually.
He has a charming way of illustrating how crowd psychology drives market cycles, which he calls the cocktail party theory.
Picture yourself at a party, standing near the punch bowl, talking to people.

In the first stage, when the market has been down for a while and everyone is gloomy, if you mention that you are a stock investor, people drift away and would rather talk to the dentist about their teeth than talk to you about stocks.
That is the stage when stocks are cheap and it is a wonderful time to buy.
In the next stage, after the market has risen a bit, people will linger long enough to tell you how risky the market is before they move on.
In the third stage, with the market up substantially, the crowd gathers around you, ignoring the dentist, eager to hear which stocks they should buy.
And in the final, dangerous stage, the crowd is crowding around you, but now they are the ones telling you which stocks you should buy, and even the dentist is giving you hot tips.
When everyone at the party has become a stock expert and is telling the professional what to buy, Lynch says, the market has very likely reached a top and is due for trouble.
It is a funny, human, and surprisingly accurate way to read where we are in the cycle, by listening to how ordinary people talk about stocks.
He tells another story in this spirit, sometimes called the man who bought Subaru.
The idea is a cautionary tale about the difference between loving a product and understanding the whole investment.
Being thrilled with your Subaru, or your Honda, is a fine lead, but it is not enough.
You still have to look at the company behind it, the finances, the competition, the price of the stock, the whole picture.
The product is the beginning of the investigation, never the conclusion.
Round and round we come, back to that same core discipline, the observation is the lead, the research is the decision.
And finally, Lynch gives us one of the most quoted and most useful warnings in all of investing, about a fatal habit that destroys returns.
He says too many investors engage in watering the weeds and pulling the flowers.
Picture your portfolio as a garden.
The flowers are your winners, the stocks that are doing beautifully, whose stories are still unfolding, that are climbing and climbing.
The weeds are your losers, the stocks that are sinking, whose stories have soured.
The natural human temptation, the thing our nervous, loss averse minds push us to do, is exactly backwards.
We rush to sell the winners, because it feels so good to lock in a profit and we are afraid of giving it back, and so we pull our flowers when they are just beginning to bloom.

And we hold onto the losers, because we cannot bear to admit a mistake and we keep hoping they will come back to even, and so we water our weeds, pouring more time and sometimes more money into the very stocks we should let go.
Lynch says do the opposite.

Cut the weeds, the businesses whose stories have genuinely broken, and let the flowers grow, hold onto your winners as long as the story stays intact, because those winners, the ones you keep watering and pruning while everyone else is selling, are the ones that become your tenbaggers.
Selling your winners too soon is how you turn a spectacular return into a merely decent one.
Some of Lynch's greatest gains came from stocks he held through years of growth while less patient investors sold out after the first double.
So let me gather all of this together for you, because the beauty of Lynch is that his wisdom actually fits in your head and in your daily life.
The amateur has an edge because you see great companies in the ordinary world before Wall Street does.
Invest in what you know, but treat that knowledge as a starting lead and always do the homework on the fundamentals before you buy.
Do not be intimidated by the professionals, who are hobbled by rules and career fears you do not share, and do not believe the theory that says you cannot win.
Understand the tenbagger, and know that you only need a few great winners in a lifetime, so you can afford to be wrong often as long as you let your winners run.
Categorize every company into one of the six types before you judge it, slow grower, stalwart, fast grower, cyclical, turnaround, or asset play, and hold each to the right standard.
Master the two minute drill, so you can always explain in plain words why you own something and what has to happen for it to work.

Learn to love the dull, the neglected, the unglamorous companies with niches and repeat customers and insiders buying, and learn to avoid the hot stock, the next big thing, the whisper story, and the company that diworseifies.
Check the handful of numbers that matter, above all the price to earnings ratio compared to the growth rate.
Sell when the story changes, not when the price twitches.
Ignore the economic forecasts and the daily noise.
And never, ever water your weeds and pull your flowers.
Now, before we finish, I promised you something concrete, a set of things you can actually do this week to begin putting Peter Lynch to work in your own life.
So let us make it real.
First, this week, become a conscious observer of your own everyday world.
Carry a small notebook or open a note on your phone, and start writing down the companies behind the products and services you and the people around you genuinely love and use.
The store you can never find parking at.
The gadget your coworkers all bought.
The brand your kids will not stop asking for.
The tool that made your job easier this year.
You are building a watchlist of leads from real life, exactly the way Lynch built his.
Second, pick just one company off that list, the one you understand best, and do a proper two minute drill on it.

Write down, in plain language you could say out loud, what the company does, which of the six categories it belongs to, why it might be a good investment, and what would have to keep happening for it to work.
If you cannot fill that out, you have learned something valuable already, and you move to the next name on your list.
Third, look up two simple numbers for that company, its price to earnings ratio and its earnings growth rate, and compare them the way Lynch taught you.
Is the P E roughly in line with the growth, or below it, which would be encouraging, or far above it, which would be a warning.
This single comparison, done honestly, puts you ahead of most casual investors.
Fourth, glance at the balance sheet for one thing, the debt.
Is this a company with a manageable amount of debt and some cash on hand, or one that is drowning in what it owes.
You are just checking whether it has the staying power to survive a rough patch.
That one look would have told you a great deal about Chrysler in its dark days.
Fifth, make a quiet promise to yourself about noise.
This week, when you hear a scary market headline or a confident prediction about where the economy is going, practice letting it pass without acting on it.
Notice how little any of it has to do with whether the specific companies you are studying are growing their earnings.
And sixth, if you already own stocks, take an honest walk through your own garden.
For each holding, ask the Lynch question, has the story changed, or has only the price changed.
Notice whether you have been tempted to sell a winner just because it is up, or to cling to a loser just because you cannot admit the mistake.
Resolve, starting now, to cut the true weeds and let the real flowers grow.
That is the program.
It is not complicated, it does not require a finance degree, and it does not ask you to predict the future.

It asks you to pay attention to the world you already live in, to do a modest amount of honest homework, to think clearly about what kind of company you are looking at, to buy good businesses at fair prices, and then to have the patience and the discipline to let your winners run while the crowd panics over nothing.
Peter Lynch proved, over thirteen extraordinary years, that this humble, human approach can outperform almost everyone.

And his enduring gift to you, the whole reason he sat down to write One Up On Wall Street, is his complete conviction that you, with your ordinary eyes and your ordinary life, already have one up on Wall Street.
You just have to start using what you know.
Go find your flowers, and let them grow.
